Comment: reference-class quadplane on a RealFlight custom model. The "px4" values are HIL_ACTUATOR_CONTROLS.controls[] indices and MUST match PWM_MAIN_FUNC* in ROMFS/px4fmu_common/init.d-posix/airframes/1202_flightaxis_quadplane (and HIL_ACT_FUNC* in the .hil twin): controls[0]=aileron left, [1]=elevator, [2]=forward/pusher motor [0,1], [3]=rudder, [4..7]=lift motors 1-4 [0,1], [8]=aileron right (surfaces are [-1,1]). DESIGN RULE: the "rf" and "px4" columns below are deliberately IDENTICAL - RealFlight channel N is driven by PX4 output channel N. This table carries no renumbering at all; the ordering decision lives entirely in PWM_MAIN_FUNC*/HIL_ACT_FUNC*, which are chosen so that controls[] comes out in the RealFlight model's own channel order. If someone changes those params, restore identity by changing the params back - do NOT re-scramble this table to compensate.
AileronComment: LEAVING rf8 (RF ch9) UNMAPPED IS DELIBERATE AND COSTS NOTHING. The airframe declares split ailerons (CA_SV_CS0=left -> controls[0], CA_SV_CS1=right -> controls[8]), but the two allocator outputs are EXACT MIRRORS: measured in SITL after a transition to fixed wing, actuator_servos.control was [0.45138, -0.45138, ...] and stayed an exact negation at every sample. CS1 carries no information CS0 does not, and CS0 already swings the FULL +-1 range rather than half of it (PX4's pseudo-inverse of the 1x2 roll row [-0.5, +0.5] is [-1; +1]), so the left aileron alone drives a single mixed RealFlight aileron channel at full roll authority. This matches ArduPilot, whose plane default is ONE combined aileron on ch1 (ardupilot/ArduPlane/Parameters.cpp:1414-1417) passed straight through by SIM_FlightAxis.cpp. RF ch9 consequently reads UnmappedDefault 0.5 (verified: constant 0.5 across every frame of a SITL run, in both hover and fixed-wing mode). ONLY add a row if your RF model genuinely drives the two ailerons on separate channels; controls[8] is parked on rf8 by the FUNC assignment precisely so that row can stay an identity row, {"rf": 8, "px4": 8, "scale": "bipolar", "reverse": <see below>}. Choose "reverse" by watching the aircraft rather than assuming: RealFlight models differ in whether the second aileron channel is reversed inside the model's own servo setup, so both conventions exist. Correct is the value that makes the two surfaces move in OPPOSITE directions; if they both deflect the same way the roll cancels out and you get a pitch/drag upset instead, so flip "reverse".
OptionsComment: Do NOT add "Rev4Servos": it swaps rf0-3 with rf4-7 wholesale. The airframe now deliberately places the control surfaces on controls[0..3] and the four lift motors on controls[4..7] so that they line up with the RealFlight model's channel order, so enabling the swap would drive the control surfaces with lift-motor throttles and the lift motors with surface deflections.
Rev4ServosComment: If Rev4Servos is ever enabled on some other model, give every row it covers an explicit "disarm" rather than relying on the -1 "hold last" default, and get_FAbridge_params.py will insist on it. The bridge itself is no longer vulnerable: buildChannels() applies the swap to a scratch copy and writes only out[], so the persistent channels[] keeps untransformed values and a held slot genuinely holds. When the swap was applied in place on channels[] every frame, a held slot was re-swapped instead of held. Being its own inverse it did not diverge the way HeliDemix did; instead a held channel ping-ponged between rf i and rf i+4 on alternate frames, a full-amplitude servo buzz at frame rate (~250 Hz). Armed, fully-mapped rows were unaffected because the fresh write each frame dominates, and unmapped slots are re-seeded from UnmappedDefault, so only hold-last rows were ever exposed.
RfModel: informational only - aircraft is selected inside RealFlight
